title: Valencia CF

infobox.key: Valencia Club de Fútbol, S. A. D.
infobox.value: (Spanish: [[baˈlenθja ˈkluβ ðe ˈfuðβol]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); [Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language"): *València Club de Futbol* [[vaˈlensi.a ˈklub de fubˈbɔl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Catalan "Help:IPA/Catalan")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) commonly referred to as **Valencia CF** or simply **Valencia**, is a Spanish professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"), Spain, that currently plays in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), the top tier of the [Spanish league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Valencia was founded in 1919 and has played its home games at the 49,430-seater [Mestalla](/wiki/Mestalla_Stadium "Mestalla Stadium") since its opening in 1923.[[2]](#cite_note-mestalla-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Association football club in Spain

For other uses, see [Valencia Football Club](/wiki/Valencia_Football_Club_(disambiguation) "Valencia Football Club (disambiguation)").

Football club

Valencia

**Valencia Club de Fútbol, S. A. D.** (Spanish: [[baˈlenθja ˈkluβ ðe ˈfuðβol]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); [Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language"): *València Club de Futbol* [[vaˈlensi.a ˈklub de fubˈbɔl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Catalan "Help:IPA/Catalan")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) commonly referred to as **Valencia CF** or simply **Valencia**, is a Spanish professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"), Spain, that currently plays in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), the top tier of the [Spanish league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Valencia was founded in 1919 and has played its home games at the 49,430-seater [Mestalla](/wiki/Mestalla_Stadium "Mestalla Stadium") since its opening in 1923.[[2]](#cite_note-mestalla-2)

Valencia has won six La Liga titles, eight [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Spanish_Cup "Spanish Cup") titles, one [Supercopa de España](/wiki/Spanish_Super_Cup "Spanish Super Cup"), and one [Copa Eva Duarte](/wiki/Copa_Eva_Duarte "Copa Eva Duarte"). In European competitions, they have won two [Inter-Cities Fairs Cups](/wiki/Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "Inter-Cities Fairs Cup"), one [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"), one [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), two [UEFA Super Cups](/wiki/UEFA_Super_Cup "UEFA Super Cup"), and one [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"). They have also reached two consecutive [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") finals ([2000](/wiki/2000_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2000 UEFA Champions League Final") and [2001](/wiki/2001_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2001 UEFA Champions League Final")). The [IFFHS](/wiki/IFFHS "IFFHS") named [World's Best Club](/wiki/IFFHS_World%27s_Best_Club "IFFHS World's Best Club") to Valencia in 2004. Valencia were also members of the [G-14](/wiki/G-14 "G-14") group of leading European football clubs and since its end has been part of the original members of the [European Club Association](/wiki/European_Club_Association "European Club Association").

Five former members of the club have been inducted into the FIFA International Football Hall of Fame, a project dedicated to preserving the memory of important figures in football history. These include [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano"), [Mario Alberto Kempes](/wiki/Mario_Alberto_Kempes "Mario Alberto Kempes"), [Romário](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1rio "Romário"), [Jorge Valdano](/wiki/Jorge_Valdano "Jorge Valdano") and [Didier Deschamps](/wiki/Didier_Deschamps "Didier Deschamps"). Valencia also has four personalities in the [FIFA 100](/wiki/FIFA_100 "FIFA 100"), its induction taking place in 2004 as part of the centenary celebrations of FIFA's creation. The *ches* club is the team with the most [Zarra Trophy](/wiki/Zarra_Trophy "Zarra Trophy") winners (5), the fourth in the [Zamora Trophy](/wiki/Zamora_Trophy "Zamora Trophy") (9) and fifth in the [Pichichi Trophy](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy") (6) at the national level, at the international level it's the third Spanish team with the most [FIFA World Player](/wiki/FIFA_World_Player "FIFA World Player") nominees (9) and the fourth in the [Ballon d'Or](/wiki/Ballon_d%27Or "Ballon d'Or") (23), it has ten nominations for the [Golden Boy Award](/wiki/Golden_Boy_Award "Golden Boy Award"), one for the 2019 [Kopa Trophy](/wiki/Kopa_Trophy "Kopa Trophy") with [Lee Kang-in](/wiki/Lee_Kang-in "Lee Kang-in") and one for the 2024 [Yashin Trophy](/wiki/Yashin_Trophy "Yashin Trophy") with [Giorgi Mamardashvili](/wiki/Giorgi_Mamardashvili "Giorgi Mamardashvili"). It has been included three times in the [UEFA Team of the Year](/wiki/UEFA_Team_of_the_Year "UEFA Team of the Year"), with [Santiago Cañizares](/wiki/Santiago_Ca%C3%B1izares "Santiago Cañizares") and [Kily González](/wiki/Kily_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Kily González") in 2001 and [David Villa](/wiki/David_Villa "David Villa") in 2010, the last repeating in the [FIFPro World XI](/wiki/FIFPro_World_XI "FIFPro World XI") in the same year.

Four Valencia players were part of the [Spanish national team](/wiki/Spanish_football_national_team "Spanish football national team") that won the [2010 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup"): David Villa, who won the Silver Boot as the second-highest scorer, tied with [Thomas Müller](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCller "Thomas Müller") on five goals and the Bronze Ball as the third best player in the final phase of the championship, [Carlos Marchena](/wiki/Carlos_Marchena "Carlos Marchena"), [David Silva](/wiki/David_Silva "David Silva") and [Juan Mata](/wiki/Juan_Mata "Juan Mata"). Seven of its members have managed to win [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") medals throughout its history: [David Albelda](/wiki/David_Albelda "David Albelda") and [Miguel Ángel Angulo](/wiki/Miguel_%C3%81ngel_Angulo "Miguel Ángel Angulo"), silver in Sydney 2000; [Fabián Ayala](/wiki/Fabi%C3%A1n_Ayala "Fabián Ayala"), gold in Athens 2004; [Éver Banega](/wiki/%C3%89ver_Banega "Éver Banega"), gold in Beijing 2008; [Carlos Soler](/wiki/Carlos_Soler_(footballer) "Carlos Soler (footballer)"), silver in Tokyo 2020; [Cristhian Mosquera](/wiki/Cristhian_Mosquera "Cristhian Mosquera") and [Diego López](/wiki/Diego_L%C3%B3pez_(footballer,_born_May_2002) "Diego López (footballer, born May 2002)"), gold in Paris 2024.

The club maintains a youth academy, known as "*Acadèmia*". Products of the academy include players such as [Miguel Tendillo](/wiki/Miguel_Tendillo "Miguel Tendillo"), [Ricardo Arias](/wiki/Ricardo_Arias_(footballer) "Ricardo Arias (footballer)"), [Fernando Gómez](/wiki/Fernando_G%C3%B3mez_Colomer "Fernando Gómez Colomer"), [Andrés Palop](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Palop "Andrés Palop"), [Javier Farinos](/wiki/Javier_Farinos "Javier Farinos"), [Raúl Albiol](/wiki/Ra%C3%BAl_Albiol "Raúl Albiol"), David Albelda, [Vicente Rodríguez](/wiki/Vicente_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Vicente Rodríguez"), [Gaizka Mendieta](/wiki/Gaizka_Mendieta "Gaizka Mendieta") and David Silva. Current stars of the game to have graduated in recent years include [Isco](/wiki/Isco "Isco"), [Jordi Alba](/wiki/Jordi_Alba "Jordi Alba"), [Paco Alcácer](/wiki/Paco_Alc%C3%A1cer "Paco Alcácer"), [Juan Bernat](/wiki/Juan_Bernat "Juan Bernat"), [José Gayà](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Gay%C3%A0 "José Gayà"), Carlos Soler, [Ferran Torres](/wiki/Ferran_Torres "Ferran Torres"), [Lee Kang-in](/wiki/Lee_Kang-in "Lee Kang-in"), Cristhian Mosquera, and [Javi Guerra](/wiki/Javi_Guerra_(footballer,_born_2003) "Javi Guerra (footballer, born 2003)").

Historically one of the biggest clubs in the world in terms of number of associates (registered paying supporters), with around 50,000 season ticket holders[[4]](#cite_note-4) at their peak, the club began to decline in the mid-2010s. Singaporean billionaire [Peter Lim](/wiki/Peter_Lim "Peter Lim") acquired the team in 2014.[[5]](#cite_note-:22-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Valencia CF](/wiki/History_of_Valencia_CF "History of Valencia CF")

The club was established on 5 March 1919 and officially approved on 18 March 1919, with Octavio Augusto Milego Díaz as its first president; incidentally, the presidency was decided by a [coin toss](/wiki/Coin_toss "Coin toss"). The club played its first competitive match away from home on 21 May 1919 against Valencia Gimnástico, and lost the match 1–0.

Valencia moved into the [Mestalla Stadium](/wiki/Mestalla_Stadium "Mestalla Stadium") in 1923, having played its home matches at the Algirós ground since 7 December 1919. The first match at Mestalla pitted the home side against Castellón Castalia and ended in a 0–0 draw. In another match the day after, Valencia won 1–0 against the same opposition. Valencia CF won the Regional Championship in 1923, and was eligible to play in the domestic [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") cup competition for the first time in its history.

### 1940s: Emergence as a giant in Spanish football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1940s: Emergence as a giant in Spanish football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Valencia_cf_festejando_con_la_copa_1941.jpg/250px-Valencia_cf_festejando_con_la_copa_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valencia_cf_festejando_con_la_copa_1941.jpg)

Players of Valencia celebrating after having won the 1941 Copa del Rey final

The [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") halted the progress of the Valencia team until 1941, when it won the Copa del Rey, beating [RCD Espanyol](/wiki/RCD_Espanyol "RCD Espanyol") in the final. In the 1941–42 season, the club won its first Spanish [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") championship title, although winning the Copa del Rey was more reputable than the championship at that time. The club maintained its consistency to capture the league title again in the 1943–44 season, as well as the 1946–47 league edition. They would conclude their decade of success by winning the 1949 Copa del Rey; this meant Valencia ended the decade with a record of three La Liga and two Copa del Rey titles. This success would help cement the club's name in Spanish football.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Faas_Wilkes_1955.jpg/250px-Faas_Wilkes_1955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faas_Wilkes_1955.jpg)

[Faas Wilkes](/wiki/Faas_Wilkes "Faas Wilkes") in 1955

In the 1950s, Valencia failed to emulate the success of the previous decade, even though it grew as a club. A restructuring of Mestalla resulted in an increase in spectator capacity to 45,000, while the club had a number of Spanish and foreign stars. Players such as [Spanish international](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") [Antonio Puchades](/wiki/Antonio_Puchades "Antonio Puchades") and [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") forward [Faas Wilkes](/wiki/Faas_Wilkes "Faas Wilkes") graced the pitch at Mestalla. In the 1952–53 season, the club finished as runners-up in La Liga, and in the following season, won the Copa del Rey, then known as the Copa del Generalísimo.

### 1960s: European successes in the Fairs Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s: European successes in the Fairs Cup")]

While managing average league form in the early 1960s, Valencia had its first European success in the form of the [Inter-Cities Fairs Cup](/wiki/Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "Inter-Cities Fairs Cup") (the forerunner to the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League")), defeating [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in the final of the [1961–62 edition](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "1961–62 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup"). The [following edition](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "1962–63 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup") of the tournament pitted Valencia against [Croatian](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") club [Dinamo Zagreb](/wiki/GNK_Dinamo_Zagreb "GNK Dinamo Zagreb") in the final, which the Spanish side also won. Valencia reached a third consecutive Inter-Cities Fairs Cup final in the [following season](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "1963–64 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup"), but this time were defeated 2–1 by fellow Spanish club [Zaragoza](/wiki/Real_Zaragoza "Real Zaragoza").

### 1970s to early 1980s: More domestic and European glory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s to early 1980s: More domestic and European glory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6d/Kempes_Valencia_CF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kempes_Valencia_CF.jpg)

[Mario Kempes](/wiki/Mario_Kempes "Mario Kempes") in 1982

Former two-time [European Footballer of the Year](/wiki/European_Footballer_of_the_Year "European Footballer of the Year") award winner [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano") was hired as Valencia coach in 1970, and immediately inspired his new club to their fourth La Liga championship and first since 1947. This secured Valencia its first qualification for the prestigious [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), contested by the various European domestic champions. Valencia reached the third round of the [1971–72 competition](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_European_Cup "1971–72 European Cup") before losing both legs to [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") champions [Újpesti Dózsa](/wiki/%C3%9Ajpesti_D%C3%B3zsa "Újpesti Dózsa"). In 1972 the club also finished runners-up both in La Liga and the domestic cup, losing to Real Madrid and Atlético Madrid, respectively. The most notable players of the 1970s era include [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") midfielder [Kurt Jara](/wiki/Kurt_Jara "Kurt Jara"), forward [Johnny Rep](/wiki/Johnny_Rep "Johnny Rep") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Argentinian](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") forward [Mario Kempes](/wiki/Mario_Kempes "Mario Kempes"), who was consecutively [La Liga top scorer](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy") in 1976–77 and 1977–78. Valencia would go on to win the Copa del Rey again in the 1978–79 season, and also capture the [European Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "European Cup Winners' Cup") the next season, after beating [English](/wiki/England "England") club [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") in the final, and the [European Super Cup](/wiki/1980_European_Super_Cup "1980 European Super Cup") against [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") thanks to the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule"), with Kempes spearheading their success in Europe.

### Mid to late 1980s: Stagnation and relegation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mid to late 1980s: Stagnation and relegation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Inauguraci%C3%B3_del_Camp_de_la_Forana_%28Alginet%2C_Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0%2C_1980%29.jpg/250px-Inauguraci%C3%B3_del_Camp_de_la_Forana_%28Alginet%2C_Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0%2C_1980%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inauguraci%C3%B3_del_Camp_de_la_Forana_(Alginet,_Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0,_1980).jpg)

Line-up in a friendly match in [Alginet](/wiki/Alginet "Alginet"), August 1980. Up: [Felman](/wiki/Dar%C3%ADo_Felman "Darío Felman"), [Manzanedo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Manzanedo "José Luis Manzanedo"), [Orlando Giménez](/wiki/Orlando_Gim%C3%A9nez "Orlando Giménez"), [Cerveró](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Cerver%C3%B3 "José Cerveró"), [Vilarrodà](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_Vilarrod%C3%A1&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pedro Vilarrodá (page does not exist)"), [Subirats](/wiki/Javier_Subirats "Javier Subirats"), [Carrete](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Carrete "José Carrete"), and [Arias](/wiki/Ricardo_Arias_(footballer) "Ricardo Arias (footballer)"). Down: [Kempes](/wiki/Mario_Alberto_Kempes "Mario Alberto Kempes"), [Morena](/wiki/Fernando_Morena "Fernando Morena"), and [Sol](/wiki/Juan_Cruz_Sol "Juan Cruz Sol").

In 1982, the club appointed [Miljan Miljanić](/wiki/Miljan_Miljani%C4%87 "Miljan Miljanić") as coach. After a disappointing season, Valencia was in 17th place and faced [relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") with seven games left to play. Koldo Aguirre replaced Miljanić as coach, and Valencia barely avoided relegation that year, relying on favorable results from other teams to ensure their own survival. In the 1983–84 and 1984–85 seasons, the club was heavily in debt under the presidency of Vicente Tormo. The club finally hit rock bottom when it was relegated at the end of the 1985–86 season, and riven with internal problems such as unpaid player and staff wages, as well as poor morale. The club was relegated for the first time after 55 years in Spanish top-flight football.

Arturo Tuzón was named the new club president, and he helped steer Valencia back to La Liga. [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano") returned as coach in 1986 and Valencia won [promotion](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") again following the 1986–87 season. Di Stéfano stayed on as coach until the 1987–88 season, when the team finished in 14th position in La Liga. [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") forward [Luboslav Penev](/wiki/Luboslav_Penev "Luboslav Penev") joined the club in 1989, as Valencia aimed to consolidate their place in La Liga. In the [1988–89 La Liga](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_La_Liga "1988–89 La Liga") season, Valencia finished third, which would signal their competitiveness going into the 1990s.

### 1990s: Re-emergence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1990s: Re-emergence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Fernando_G%C3%B3mez_Colomer.jpeg/250px-Fernando_G%C3%B3mez_Colomer.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Fernando_G%C3%B3mez_Colomer.jpeg)

[Fernando Gómez Colomer](/wiki/Fernando_G%C3%B3mez_Colomer "Fernando Gómez Colomer") is the player with the most appearances for the club with 556

In the [1989–90 La Liga](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_La_Liga "1989–90 La Liga") season, Valencia finished as runners-up to Real Madrid, and thus qualified for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup").

[Guus Hiddink](/wiki/Guus_Hiddink "Guus Hiddink") was appointed as head coach in the 1991–92 season, and the club finished fourth in the League and reached the quarter-finals of the Copa del Rey. In 1992, Valencia officially became a Sporting Limited Company, and retained Hiddink as their coach until 1993.

[Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") coach [Carlos Alberto Parreira](/wiki/Carlos_Alberto_Parreira "Carlos Alberto Parreira"), fresh from winning the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") with the [Brazil national team](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), became manager at Mestalla in 1994. Parreira immediately signed Spanish goalkeeper [Andoni Zubizarreta](/wiki/Andoni_Zubizarreta "Andoni Zubizarreta"), [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") forward [Oleg Salenko](/wiki/Oleg_Salenko "Oleg Salenko"), and [Predrag Mijatović](/wiki/Predrag_Mijatovi%C4%87 "Predrag Mijatović"), but failed to produce results expected of him. He was replaced by new coach [José Manuel Rielo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Rielo "José Manuel Rielo"). The club's earlier successes continued to elude it, although it was not short of top coaching staff like [Luis Aragonés](/wiki/Luis_Aragon%C3%A9s "Luis Aragonés") and [Jorge Valdano](/wiki/Jorge_Valdano "Jorge Valdano"), as well as foreign star forwards like Brazilian [Romário](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1rio "Romário"), [Claudio López](/wiki/Claudio_L%C3%B3pez_(footballer) "Claudio López (footballer)"), [Ariel Ortega](/wiki/Ariel_Ortega "Ariel Ortega") from Argentina, and [Adrian Ilie](/wiki/Adrian_Ilie "Adrian Ilie") from Romania. In the [1995–96 La Liga](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_La_Liga "1995–96 La Liga") season, Valencia finished second to [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid"), being unable to capture the title after a close fought race.

Valencia would struggle for the next two seasons, but the [1998–99 La Liga](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_La_Liga "1998–99 La Liga") season would signal the start of one of the club's most successful periods in their history; they lifted their first trophy in nineteen years by winning the [1998–99 Copa del Rey](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Copa_del_Rey "1998–99 Copa del Rey") under [Claudio Ranieri](/wiki/Claudio_Ranieri "Claudio Ranieri"), and also qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League").

### 2000s: Valencia returns to the top of Spanish and European football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000s: Valencia returns to the top of Spanish and European football")]

Valencia started the 1999–2000 season by winning another title, beating [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") in the [Spanish Super Cup](/wiki/1999_Supercopa_de_Espa%C3%B1a "1999 Supercopa de España"). Valencia finished third in the league, four points behind champions [Deportivo La Coruña](/wiki/Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Deportivo de La Coruña"), and level on points with second-placed Barça. The biggest success for the club, however, was in the Champions League; for the first time in its history, Valencia reached the [European Cup](/wiki/European_Champion_Clubs%27_Cup "European Champion Clubs' Cup") final. However, in the [final](/wiki/2000_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2000 UEFA Champions League final") played in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") on 24 May 2000, [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") would beat Valencia 3–0.

The final would also be Claudio López's farewell, as he had agreed to sign for Italian side [Lazio](/wiki/SS_Lazio "SS Lazio"); also leaving was Farinós for [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") and Gerard for Barcelona. The notable signings of that summer were [John Carew](/wiki/John_Carew "John Carew"), [Rubén Baraja](/wiki/Rub%C3%A9n_Baraja "Rubén Baraja"), [Roberto Ayala](/wiki/Roberto_Ayala "Roberto Ayala"), [Vicente Rodríguez](/wiki/Vicente_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Vicente Rodríguez"), and Brazilian left-back [Fábio Aurélio](/wiki/F%C3%A1bio_Aur%C3%A9lio "Fábio Aurélio"). That season Valencia also bought [Pablo Aimar](/wiki/Pablo_Aimar "Pablo Aimar") in the winter transfer window. Baraja, Aimar, Vicente, and Ayala would soon become a staple of Valencia's dominance of the early 2000s in La Liga.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg/250px-Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg)

[2000 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2000_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2000 UEFA Champions League Final") starting lineup

Valencia started the championship on the right foot and were top of the league after ten games. After the Christmas break, however, Valencia started to pay for the top demand that such a draining competition like the Champions League requires. After passing the two mini-league phases, [Héctor Cúper](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_C%C3%BAper "Héctor Cúper")'s team eliminated English sides [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") in the quarter-finals and [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C "Leeds United F.C") in the semi-finals, reaching the [final](/wiki/2001_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2001 UEFA Champions League final") for the second consecutive year. In the final match against [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich"), played in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") at the [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro") on 23 May, [Gaizka Mendieta](/wiki/Gaizka_Mendieta "Gaizka Mendieta") gave Valencia the lead by scoring from the penalty spot right at the start of the match. Goalkeeper [Santiago Cañizares](/wiki/Santiago_Ca%C3%B1izares "Santiago Cañizares") then stopped a penalty from [Mehmet Scholl](/wiki/Mehmet_Scholl "Mehmet Scholl"), but [Stefan Effenberg](/wiki/Stefan_Effenberg "Stefan Effenberg") drew Bayern level after the break thanks to another penalty. After extra time, the match went to a penalty shoot-out, where a [Mauricio Pellegrino](/wiki/Mauricio_Pellegrino "Mauricio Pellegrino") miss gave Bayern Champions League glory and dealt Valencia a second-straight defeat in the final. Valencia went on to slip to fifth place in La Liga and out of the Champions League positions for the 2001–02 season. Going into the final league match, Valencia only needed a draw at the [Camp Nou](/wiki/Camp_Nou "Camp Nou") against Barcelona to seal Champions League qualification. However, *Los Che* lost to Barcelona 3–2, with a last minute goal completing a [hat-trick](/wiki/Hat-trick "Hat-trick") from [Rivaldo](/wiki/Rivaldo "Rivaldo"), resulting in Barcelona qualifying for the Champions League ahead of their side.

Valencia president D. Pedro Cortés resigned for personal reasons and left the club in July, with the satisfaction of overseeing the club win the Copa del Rey and Spanish Super Cup, as well as reaching two successive Champions League finals. D. Jaime Ortí replaced Cortés as president and expressed his intention of maintaining the good form that had made the club so admired on the European circuit. There were also some changes in the team and staff. [Rafael Benítez](/wiki/Rafael_Ben%C3%ADtez "Rafael Benítez"), after helping [Tenerife](/wiki/CD_Tenerife "CD Tenerife") to promotion, replaced Héctor Cúper after the latter became the new coach at Inter in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). Among the playing squad, Gaizka Mendieta, [Didier Deschamps](/wiki/Didier_Deschamps "Didier Deschamps"), [Luis Milla](/wiki/Luis_Milla_(footballer,_born_1966) "Luis Milla (footballer, born 1966)"), and [Zlatko Zahovič](/wiki/Zlatko_Zahovi%C4%8D "Zlatko Zahovič") left, while [Carlos Marchena](/wiki/Carlos_Marchena "Carlos Marchena"), [Mista](/wiki/Mista_(footballer) "Mista (footballer)"), [Curro Torres](/wiki/Curro_Torres "Curro Torres"), [Francisco Rufete](/wiki/Francisco_Rufete "Francisco Rufete"), [Gonzalo de los Santos](/wiki/Gonzalo_de_los_Santos "Gonzalo de los Santos"), and [Salva Ballesta](/wiki/Salva_Ballesta "Salva Ballesta") all arrived.

From 1999 up until the end of the 2004 season, Valencia had one of their most successful periods in the club's history. With a total of two La Liga titles, a [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"), a Copa del Rey, and a UEFA Super Cup in those six years, no less than five first class titles and two Champions League finals had been achieved.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Roberto_Ayala_y_Javier_Zanetti_-_07FEB2007_-_Francia_-_presidencia-govar.jpg/250px-Roberto_Ayala_y_Javier_Zanetti_-_07FEB2007_-_Francia_-_presidencia-govar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roberto_Ayala_y_Javier_Zanetti_-_07FEB2007_-_Francia_-_presidencia-govar.jpg)

During Valencia's domestic and European dominance of the early 2000s, Argentine [Roberto Ayala](/wiki/Roberto_Ayala "Roberto Ayala") had been a key component in their defense

That first match against fellow title rivals Real Madrid produced a significant and important victory. This was followed by a record of eleven consecutive wins, breaking their existing record set in the 1970–71 season, which was also the club's La Liga title win under [Alfredo Di Stéfano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_St%C3%A9fano "Alfredo Di Stéfano").

After a defeat in [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña") against [Deportivo](/wiki/Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Deportivo de La Coruña") on 9 December 2001, the team had to overcome [Espanyol](/wiki/RCD_Espanyol "RCD Espanyol") at the [Estadi Olímpic Lluís Companys](/wiki/Estadi_Ol%C3%ADmpic_Llu%C3%ADs_Companys "Estadi Olímpic Lluís Companys") to avoid further backsliding behind the league leaders. at half-time, Valencia were 2–0 down, but a comeback in the second half saw them win 3–2.

In the second part of the season, Benítez's team suffered a temporary setback after losing 1–0 at the [Santiago Bernabéu](/wiki/Santiago_Bernab%C3%A9u_Stadium "Santiago Bernabéu Stadium") to Real Madrid, but in the coming six matches they recovered from this defeat and achieved four victories and two draws.

[2001 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2001_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2001 UEFA Champions League Final") starting lineup

In one of these crucial games against Espanyol, Valencia were trailing 1–0 at half-time and down a player as well following the dismissal of Carboni. However, after a second half brace from Rubén Baraja, they would achieve a 2–1 comeback win. Furthermore, Real Madrid's defeat at the [Anoeta](/wiki/Anoeta_Stadium "Anoeta Stadium") to [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad "Real Sociedad") left Valencia with a three-point lead at the top of the table.

Valencia's final game of the season was on 5 May 2002 at [La Rosaleda](/wiki/La_Rosaleda "La Rosaleda") against [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga_CF "Málaga CF"), a day that has gone down in Valencia's history. The team shut itself away in [Benalmádena](/wiki/Benalm%C3%A1dena "Benalmádena"), close to the scene of the game, in order to gain focus. An early goal from Roberto Ayala and another close to half-time from Fábio Aurélio secured Valencia a fifth La Liga crown, 31 years after their last title win.

The 2002–03 season was a disappointing one for Valencia, as they failed in their attempt to retain the La Liga title and ended up outside of the Champions League spots in fifth, behind [Celta Vigo](/wiki/RC_Celta_de_Vigo "RC Celta de Vigo"). They were also knocked out in the quarter-finals of the Champions League by Inter Milan on away goals. The 2003–04 season saw Valencia trailing longtime leaders Real Madrid. In February, with 26 matches played, Madrid were eight points clear at the top of the table.[[7]](#cite_note-7) However, their form severely declined in the late stage of the season, and consecutive losses in their last five games of the campaign allowed Valencia to overtake them and claim the title, their second in three seasons. The club also added the UEFA Cup to this success, defeating [Marseille](/wiki/Olympique_de_Marseille "Olympique de Marseille") 2–0 in the [final](/wiki/2004_UEFA_Cup_final "2004 UEFA Cup final").

In the summer of 2004, manager Benítez decided to depart Valencia, stating he had had problems with the club president; he would soon become head coach of [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). He was replaced by former Valencia coach [Claudio Ranieri](/wiki/Claudio_Ranieri "Claudio Ranieri"), who had recently been sacked by [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."). Despite lifting the [European Super Cup](/wiki/2004_UEFA_Super_Cup "2004 UEFA Super Cup") after defeating [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_UEFA_Champions_League "2003–04 UEFA Champions League") winners [Porto](/wiki/FC_Porto "FC Porto"), his second reign at the club was a disappointment; Valencia harboured realistic hopes of retaining their La Liga crown but, by February, found themselves in seventh place. Valencia had also been knocked out of the Champions League group phase, with Ranieri being sacked promptly in February. The 2004–05 season ended with Valencia outside of the UEFA Cup spots.

In the summer of 2005, [Getafe](/wiki/Getafe_CF "Getafe CF") coach [Quique Flores](/wiki/Quique_Flores "Quique Flores") was appointed as the new manager of Valencia and ended the season in third place, which in turn gained Valencia a place in the Champions League after a season away from the competition. The 2006–07 season was one with many difficulties; a campaign which started with realistic hopes of challenging for the title was disrupted with a huge list of injuries to key players, as well as internal arguments between Flores and new sporting director [Amedeo Carboni](/wiki/Amedeo_Carboni "Amedeo Carboni"). Valencia ended the season in fourth place and were knocked out of the Champions League in the quarter-finals by Chelsea 3–2 on aggregate, after they had knocked out Italian champions Inter in the second round. In the summer of 2007, the internal fight between Flores and Carboni was settled, with Carboni being replaced by Ángel Ruiz as the new sporting director of Valencia.

On 29 October 2007, the Valencia board of directors fired Flores after a string of disappointing performances, and caretaker manager [Óscar Fernández](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Fern%C3%A1ndez_(football_manager,_born_1974) "Óscar Fernández (football manager, born 1974)") took over on a temporary basis until a full-time manager was found, rumoured to be either [Marcello Lippi](/wiki/Marcello_Lippi "Marcello Lippi") or [José Mourinho](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mourinho "José Mourinho"). A day later, Dutch manager [Ronald Koeman](/wiki/Ronald_Koeman "Ronald Koeman") announced he would be leaving [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") to sign for Valencia. However, Koeman's appointment failed to lead to improvement; in fact, Valencia even went on to drop to the 15th position in the league, just two points above the relegation zone. Despite their poor league form, Valencia would still go on to lift the Copa del Rey on 16 April 2008, following a 3–1 victory over Getafe at the [Vicente Calderón](/wiki/Vicente_Calder%C3%B3n_Stadium "Vicente Calderón Stadium"). This was the club's seventh Copa title. Five days later, one day after a devastating 5–1 league defeat in Bilbao, Valencia fired Koeman and replaced him with [Voro](/wiki/Voro_(footballer) "Voro (footballer)"), who would guide Valencia as caretaker manager for the remainder of the season. He went on to win the first match since the sacking of Koeman, beating [Osasuna](/wiki/CA_Osasuna "CA Osasuna") 3–0. Voro would eventually drag Valencia from the relegation battle to a safe mid-table finish of tenth place, finally ending a disastrous league campaign for *Los Che*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Manolo_Llorente.jpg/250px-Manolo_Llorente.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manolo_Llorente.jpg)

35th president of Valencia [Manuel Llorente](/wiki/Manuel_Llorente "Manuel Llorente")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Sentiment_Valencianista.jpg/330px-Sentiment_Valencianista.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sentiment_Valencianista.jpg)

Tifo at [Mestalla Stadium](/wiki/Mestalla_Stadium "Mestalla Stadium")

Highly rated [Unai Emery](/wiki/Unai_Emery "Unai Emery") was announced as the new head coach of Valencia on 22 May 2008. The start of the young manager's career looked to be promising, with the club winning four out of its first five games, a surge that saw the team rise to the top position of the La Liga table. Despite looking impressive in Europe, *Los Che* then hit a poor run of form in the league that saw them dip as low as seventh in the standings. Amid the slump emerged reports of a massive internal debt at the club exceeding 400 million euros, as well as that the players had been unpaid for weeks. The team's problems were compounded when they were knocked out of the UEFA Cup by [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") on away goals. After a run where Valencia took only five points from ten games in La Liga, an announcement was made that the club had secured a loan that would cover the players' expenses until the end of the year. This announcement coincided with an upturn in form, and the club won six of its next eight games to surge back into the critical fourth place Champions' League spot. However, *Los Che* were then pushed down to sixth place in the league following defeats to top four rivals [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") and [Villarreal](/wiki/Villarreal_CF "Villarreal CF") in two of their final three games, meaning they failed to qualify for the Champions League for a second successive season.

### 2010–2014: Debt issues and stability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2010–2014: Debt issues and stability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Albelda_2012.jpg/250px-Albelda_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Albelda_2012.jpg)

Over the course of 15 seasons and 481 official matches from 1997 to 2013, as well as serving as team captain, defensive midfielder [David Albelda](/wiki/David_Albelda "David Albelda") became one of the most recognisable players of Valencia CF.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

No solution had yet been found to address the massive debt Valencia was faced with, and rumors persisted that top talents such as [David Villa](/wiki/David_Villa "David Villa"), [Juan Mata](/wiki/Juan_Mata "Juan Mata"), and [David Silva](/wiki/David_Silva "David Silva") could leave the club to help balance the books. In the first season of the new decade, Valencia returned to the Champions League for the first time since the 2007–08 campaign, as they finished comfortably in third place in the [2009–10 La Liga](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga") standings. However, in the summer of 2010, due to financial reasons, David Villa and David Silva were sold to Barcelona and [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), respectively, to reduce the club's massive debt. Despite the loss of two of the club's most important players, the team was able to finish comfortably in third place again in the [2010–11 La Liga](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_La_Liga "2010–11 La Liga") for the second season running, although they would be eliminated from the Champions League by German side [Schalke 04](/wiki/FC_Schalke_04 "FC Schalke 04") in the round of 16. In the summer of 2011, then-captain Juan Mata was sold to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") to further help Valencia's precarious financial situation. It was announced by club president [Manuel Llorente](/wiki/Manuel_Llorente "Manuel Llorente") that the club's debt had been decreased and that the work on the new stadium would restart as soon as possible, sometime in 2012.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/DeporValencia.jpg/250px-DeporValencia.jpg)](/wiki/File:DeporValencia.jpg)

[Deportivo de La Coruña](/wiki/Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Deportivo de La Coruña") vs. Valencia CF.

During the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Valencia_CF_season "2012–13 Valencia CF season"), [Ernesto Valverde](/wiki/Ernesto_Valverde "Ernesto Valverde") was announced as the new manager, but after failing to qualify for the Champions League, he stepped down and was replaced by [Miroslav Đukić](/wiki/Miroslav_%C4%90uki%C4%87 "Miroslav Đukić"). On 5 July 2013, [Amadeo Salvo](/wiki/Amadeo_Salvo "Amadeo Salvo") was named as the new president of the club. Almost a month after Salvo was named president, on 1 August, Valencia sold star striker [Roberto Soldado](/wiki/Roberto_Soldado "Roberto Soldado") to English club [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") for a reported fee of €30 million. Đukić was sacked six months into the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Valencia_CF_season "2013–14 Valencia CF season") after just six wins in his first sixteen matches, Valencia's worst start to a season in fifteen years.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He was replaced by [Juan Antonio Pizzi](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Pizzi "Juan Antonio Pizzi") on 26 December 2013.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Under Pizzi, Valencia reached the semi-finals of the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_UEFA_Europa_League "2013–14 UEFA Europa League"), where they lost to eventual winners [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_FC "Sevilla FC") on away goals, and finished eighth in [La Liga](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_La_Liga "2013–14 La Liga") despite a disastrous start to the season.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-auto-12)

### 2014–present: Decline under Peter Lim's ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2014–present: Decline under Peter Lim's ownership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Peter_Lim.jpg/250px-Peter_Lim.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_Lim.jpg)

[Peter Lim](/wiki/Peter_Lim "Peter Lim") has owned Valencia since 2014

In May 2014, Singaporean businessman [Peter Lim](/wiki/Peter_Lim "Peter Lim") was designated by the Fundación Valencia CF as the buyer of 70.4% of the shares owned by the club's foundation.[[13]](#cite_note-auto1-13)[[14]](#cite_note-auto2-14) After months of negotiations between Lim and [Bankia](/wiki/Bankia "Bankia") (the main creditor of the club), an agreement was reached in August 2014.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Juan Antonio Pizzi was unexpectedly sacked as head coach and replaced by [Nuno Espírito Santo](/wiki/Nuno_Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Nuno Espírito Santo") on 2 July 2014.[[12]](#cite_note-auto-12)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Later, Salvo revealed in an interview that hiring Nuno was one of the conditions Lim had insisted on when buying the club. This raised eyebrows in the media because of Nuno's close relationship with the football agent [Jorge Mendes](/wiki/Jorge_Mendes "Jorge Mendes"), whose first-ever client was Nuno.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Lim and Mendes were also close friends and business partners.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Regardless, Nuno's first season was a successful one. Notable signings included [Álvaro Negredo](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Negredo "Álvaro Negredo"), [André Gomes](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Gomes "André Gomes") and [Enzo Pérez](/wiki/Enzo_P%C3%A9rez "Enzo Pérez"), who had just won the [Player of the Year](/wiki/LPFP_Primeira_Liga_Player_of_the_Year "LPFP Primeira Liga Player of the Year") in the [Portuguese Primeira Liga](/wiki/Primeira_Liga "Primeira Liga").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Valencia finished the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_La_Liga "2014–15 La Liga") in fourth place, achieving Champions League qualification with 77 points, just one point ahead of Sevilla after a dramatic final week where they defeated [Granada](/wiki/Granada_CF "Granada CF") 4–0.[[12]](#cite_note-auto-12)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On 2 July 2015, Amadeo Salvo resigned from his post as the executive president of Valencia, citing personal reasons. He was a popular figure among the fans.[[24]](#cite_note-24) On 10 August 2015, [Nicolás Otamendi](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Otamendi "Nicolás Otamendi") was sold to Manchester City for £32 million and [Aymen Abdennour](/wiki/Aymen_Abdennour "Aymen Abdennour") was signed from [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") for £22 million as his replacement.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Valencia defeated Monaco in the [Champions League play-off round](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_UEFA_Champions_League "2015–16 UEFA Champions League") with a 4–3 aggregate victory.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, Valencia had a poor start to the [2015–16 league season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_La_Liga "2015–16 La Liga"), winning only five out of thirteen matches and failing to progress from the [Champions League group stage](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_UEFA_Champions_League_group_stage "2015–16 UEFA Champions League group stage"). The fans were also increasingly concerned about the growing influence of Jorge Mendes in the club's activities.[[28]](#cite_note-28) On 29 November, Nuno resigned as manager and former [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") defender [Gary Neville](/wiki/Gary_Neville "Gary Neville") was hired as his replacement on 2 December.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Valencia went winless for nine matches before earning their first win under Neville in a 2–1 victory at home against Espanyol.[[31]](#cite_note-31) On 30 March 2016, Neville was sacked after recording the lowest win percentage in La Liga history for a Valencia manager with minimum of five matches, winning just three out of sixteen games. He was replaced by [Pako Ayestarán](/wiki/Pako_Ayestar%C3%A1n "Pako Ayestarán"), who had been brought in by Neville as the assistant coach just one month prior.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Valencia finished the season in twelfth place.

In the summer of 2016, André Gomes and [Paco Alcácer](/wiki/Paco_Alc%C3%A1cer "Paco Alcácer") were both sold to Barcelona and [Shkodran Mustafi](/wiki/Shkodran_Mustafi "Shkodran Mustafi") was sold to Arsenal, while [Ezequiel Garay](/wiki/Ezequiel_Garay "Ezequiel Garay") and former Manchester United player [Nani](/wiki/Nani_(footballer) "Nani (footballer)") were brought in.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-MustafiArsenal-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Pako Ayestarán was sacked on 21 September 2016 after four straight defeats at the beginning of the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Valencia_CF_season "2016–17 Valencia CF season"). Former [Italy national team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") head coach [Cesare Prandelli](/wiki/Cesare_Prandelli "Cesare Prandelli") was hired as his replacement on 28 September.[[39]](#cite_note-39) However, he resigned after just three months on 30 December, claiming the club had made him false transfer promises.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Days later, on 7 January 2017, Valencia sporting director [Jesús García Pitarch](/wiki/Jes%C3%BAs_Garc%C3%ADa_Pitarch "Jesús García Pitarch") also resigned, saying he felt like he was being used as a shield for criticism by the club and that he could not defend something he no longer believed in.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Voro](/wiki/Salvador_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Marco "Salvador González Marco") was named caretaker manager for the fifth time until the end of season, with Valencia in 17th position and in danger of relegation.[[43]](#cite_note-43) However, results improved under Voro and he steered Valencia clear off relegation, ultimately finishing the season in 12th place.[[44]](#cite_note-44) On 27 March, Mateu Alemany was named the new director general of Valencia.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Valencia_Club_de_F%C3%BAtbol_league_performance_1929-present.svg/330px-Valencia_Club_de_F%C3%BAtbol_league_performance_1929-present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Valencia_Club_de_F%C3%BAtbol_league_performance_1929-present.svg)

Chart of Valencia CF league performance 1929–present

The club also announced club president [Lay Hoon Chan](/wiki/Lay_Hoon_Chan "Lay Hoon Chan") had submitted her resignation and that she would be replaced by [Anil Murthy](/wiki/Anil_Murthy "Anil Murthy").[[46]](#cite_note-46) After rumors arose of Lim's attempts at selling the club, Murthy assured the fans and local media that Valencia was a long-term project for both him and Lim, and they would not consider selling the club.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) For the following season, former Villarreal coach [Marcelino](/wiki/Marcelino_Garc%C3%ADa_Toral "Marcelino García Toral") was named the new manager on 12 May.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

After a successful first season under Marcelino, the club secured fourth place in La Liga and a return to the Champions League. In Marcelino's second season, Valencia again finished fourth and also reached the semi-finals of the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Europa_League "2018–19 UEFA Europa League"). On 25 May 2019, Valencia won the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Copa_del_Rey "2018–19 Copa del Rey"), their first trophy since 2008, upsetting league winners Barcelona 2–1 in the [final](/wiki/2019_Copa_del_Rey_final "2019 Copa del Rey final").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Both Marcelino and sporting director [Mateu Alemany](/wiki/Mateu_Alemany "Mateu Alemany"), who were credited as the architects of this success,[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51) were fired on 11 September 2019 after the former publicly criticized Lim.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51) He was replaced by the ultimately unsuccessful [Albert Celades](/wiki/Albert_Celades "Albert Celades"), who was sacked due to poor results, while sporting director [César Sánchez](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_S%C3%A1nchez "César Sánchez") resigned that same season,[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51) making it six different managers and another six sporting directors by 2020.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

For the 2020–21 season, manager [Javi Gracia](/wiki/Javi_Gracia "Javi Gracia") was hired. He was put in charge of a team full of prospects and reserves after the club failed to sign any players during the summer transfer window,[[53]](#cite_note-53) but sold key players such as captain [Dani Parejo](/wiki/Dani_Parejo "Dani Parejo").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Local wonderkid [Ferran Torres](/wiki/Ferran_Torres "Ferran Torres") was also sold to [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") for a price deemed half his market value.[[5]](#cite_note-:22-5) Overall, Valencia sold players worth 85 million euros in order to rebalance the club's books.[[55]](#cite_note-:2-55) At the beginning of the season, the club was unable to pay the salaries to the remaining players.[[56]](#cite_note-56) After six seasons under Peter Lim's ownership, Valencia had accumulated losses of 323 million euros,[[57]](#cite_note-57) In the following years, the playing squad was cut significantly in terms of quality and Lim's ownership has faced strong criticism in Valencia.[[5]](#cite_note-:22-5)[[55]](#cite_note-:2-55)[[58]](#cite_note-:1-58)

In the 2021–22 season, [José Bordalás](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Bordal%C3%A1s "José Bordalás") was hired as head coach, following his five-season tenure with Getafe.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Valencia reached the [Copa del Rey final](/wiki/2022_Copa_del_Rey_final "2022 Copa del Rey final") in Bordalás' first season in charge, but lost to [Real Betis](/wiki/Real_Betis "Real Betis") on penalties following a 1–1 draw.

In June 2022, Anil Murthy left after reportedly insulting the club's owner. Peter Lim's sons became club directors and Lay Hoon Chan returned as the club President.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Mestallapanoramic4.jpg/250px-Mestallapanoramic4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mestallapanoramic4.jpg)

Panoramic of the Mestalla

Valencia played its first years at the Algirós stadium, but moved to the [Mestalla](/wiki/Mestalla_Stadium "Mestalla Stadium") in 1923. In the 1950s, the Mestalla was restructured, which resulted in a capacity increase to 45,000 spectators. Today it holds 49,430 seats, making it the fifth largest stadium in Spain. It is also renowned for its steep terracing and for being one of the most intimidating atmospheres in Europe.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Mestalla_trofeu_taronja_120811.jpg/250px-Mestalla_trofeu_taronja_120811.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mestalla_trofeu_taronja_120811.jpg)

Valencia vs. [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") at the Mestalla in 2011

On 20 May 1923, the Mestalla pitch was inaugurated with a friendly match between Valencia and [Levante UD](/wiki/Levante_UD "Levante UD").

A long history has taken place on the Mestalla field since its very beginning, when the Valencia team was not yet in the [Primera División](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"). Back then, this stadium could hold 17,000 spectators, and at that time, the club started to show its potential in regional championships, which led the managers of the time to carry out the first alterations of Mestalla in 1927. The stadium's total capacity increased to 25,000 before it became severely damaged during the Civil War; the Mestalla was used as a concentration camp and a junk warehouse. It would only keep its structure, since the rest was a lonely plot of land with no terraces and a stand broken during the war. Once the Valencian [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(sports) "Pitch (sports)") was renovated, the Mestalla stadium in which the team managed to bring home their first title in 1941.

During the 1950s, the Valencia ground experienced the deepest change in its whole history. That project resulted in a stadium with a capacity of 45,500 spectators, that eventually saw destruction by a [flood in October 1957](/wiki/1957_Valencia_flood "1957 Valencia flood") that arose from the overflowing of the [Turia River](/wiki/Turia_River "Turia River"). Nevertheless, the Mestalla not only returned to normality, but also some more improvements were added, like artificial light, which was inaugurated during the 1959 [Fallas](/wiki/Fallas "Fallas") festivities.

During the 1960s, the stadium kept the same appearance, while the [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") view around it was quickly being transformed. Moreover, the ground held its first European matches, with [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") being the first foreign team to play at the Mestalla, on 15 September 1961.

From 1969, the expression "*Anem a Mestalla*" ("Let's go to the Mestalla"), so common among the supporters, began to fall into oblivion. The reason of this was due to a proposed name change of the stadium to honor Luis Casanova Giner, the club's most successful president. Giner admitted he was completely overwhelmed by such honour, but requested in 1994 that the original name of Mestalla remained.

In 1972, the head office of the club, located in the back of the numbered terraces, was inaugurated. It consisted of an office of [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") style with a trophy hall, which held the founding flag of the club. In the summer of 1973, more goal seats, which meant the elimination of fourteen rows of standing [terraces](/wiki/Terrace_(stadium) "Terrace (stadium)"), were added to provide comfort. Club management also considered the possibility of moving the Mestalla from its present location, to land on the outskirts of the town, before deciding against it.

Mestalla also hosted the [Spain national football team](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") for the first time in 1925. It was chosen as the national team's group venue when Spain staged the [1982 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) and at the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics") held in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"). All of Spain's matches up to the final were held at Mestalla, as they won Gold.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Mestalla has been the setting for important international matches, has held several Cup finals, and has also been the home of Levante. The ground also provided a temporary home for [Castellón](/wiki/CD_Castell%C3%B3n "CD Castellón") and [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") for European games due to stadium development.

### New stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: New stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Maqueta_Nuevo_Mestalla.jpg/250px-Maqueta_Nuevo_Mestalla.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maqueta_Nuevo_Mestalla.jpg)

Model of [Nou Mestalla](/wiki/Nou_Mestalla "Nou Mestalla")

The 2008–09 season was due to be the last season at the Mestalla, with the club intending to move to their new 75,000-seater stadium [Nou Mestalla](/wiki/Nou_Mestalla "Nou Mestalla") in time for the 2009–10 season. However, due to the club being in financial crisis, work on the new stadium has been heavily delayed.[[64]](#cite_note-64) On 10 January 2025, it was reported that construction for Nou Mestalla has resumed and is set to be completed prior to the 2027–28 season.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Kit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Kit")]

### Anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Anthem")]

To celebrate the club's 75th anniversary the then president Arturo Tuzón commissioned Pablo Sánchez Torella to compose an anthem for the club. This was a *[pasodoble](/wiki/Pasodoble "Pasodoble")* whose lyrics were later written by Ramón Gimeno Gil in the [Valencian language](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language"). The anthem had its official presentation on the anniversary of the club on 21 September 1993.

### Crest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Crest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Escut_de_Val%C3%A8ncia.svg/120px-Escut_de_Val%C3%A8ncia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Escut_de_Val%C3%A8ncia.svg)

Coat of arms of the city of Valencia

Valencia and the Balearic Islands were conquered by King [James I of Aragon](/wiki/James_I_of_Aragon "James I of Aragon") during the first half of the 13th century. After the conquest, the King gave them the status of independent kingdoms of whom he was also the king (but they were independent of Aragonese laws and institutions). The arms of Valencia show those of James I.

The unique crowned letters "L" besides the shield were granted by [Peter IV](/wiki/Peter_IV_of_Aragon "Peter IV of Aragon"). The reason for the letters was that the city had been loyal twice to the King, hence twice a letter "L" and a crown for the king.

There are several possible explanations for the bat; one is that bats are simply quite common in the area. The second theory is that on 9 October 1238, when James I was about to enter the city, re-conquering it from the Moors, a bat landed on the top of his flag, which he interpreted as a good omen. Following his victory, the bat were then added to the coat of arms.

In May 2013, it was reported that [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") had started a legal case against the club, claiming that the new bat image design was too similar to [Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman").[[67]](#cite_note-67) The club issued a statement clarifying that it had intended to use a revised version of its bat logo for a line of casual clothing and applied for permission from the [Office of Harmonisation of the Internal Market](/wiki/European_Union_Intellectual_Property_Office "European Union Intellectual Property Office") but the application was dropped after DC Comics filed an *objection*, not a lawsuit.[[68]](#cite_note-68) DC Comics again filed a complaint with the EU's office of IP opposing the trademark application made by Valencia for its [centennial](/wiki/Centennial "Centennial") logo, claiming there is likely to be confusion with its Batman's symbol.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## The Academy: Training Centre Foundation Valencia CF

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: The Academy: Training Centre Foundation Valencia CF")]

Since May 2009, Valencia CF has had a training centre, this is the first multidisciplinary training center for a football club in Spain.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The Training Centre Foundation Valencia CF "The Academy" offers university education,[[74]](#cite_note-74) classroom training, and online training related to sport and football (soccer).[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Valencia CF is one of the few clubs in Spain that organises a [Sport Management](/wiki/Sport_Management "Sport Management") MBA, the MBA in International Sport Management, currently performs with [Valencia Catholic University Saint Vincent Martyr](/wiki/Valencia_Catholic_University_Saint_Vincent_Martyr "Valencia Catholic University Saint Vincent Martyr").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

On the 90th anniversary of Valencia CF, the academy opened with the University of Valencia the first university course that studied the history of a football club, Valencia CF is the first football club in Spain to be an object of study in college.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Motorsports involvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Motorsports involvement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Valencia_CF_-_Espa%C3%B1ol_2012_%5E32_-_Flickr_-_V%C3%ADctor_Guti%C3%A9rrez_Navarro.jpg/250px-Valencia_CF_-_Espa%C3%B1ol_2012_%5E32_-_Flickr_-_V%C3%ADctor_Guti%C3%A9rrez_Navarro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valencia_CF_-_Espa%C3%B1ol_2012_%5E32_-_Flickr_-_V%C3%ADctor_Guti%C3%A9rrez_Navarro.jpg)

Valencia CF fans

Valencia CF were also involved in motorsports such as [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), [Super GT](/wiki/Super_GT "Super GT"), [MotoGP](/wiki/MotoGP "MotoGP"), [Moto2](/wiki/Moto2 "Moto2"), [Moto3](/wiki/Moto3 "Moto3"), [250cc](/wiki/MotoGP "MotoGP") and [Formula Nippon](/wiki/Formula_Nippon "Formula Nippon"). Valencia CF was an official partner of [Panasonic Toyota Racing](/wiki/Toyota_Racing_(Formula_One_team) "Toyota Racing (Formula One team)") in 2003 until 2008 to commemorate Toyota as their shirt sponsor. Valencia CF also sponsored all Toyota-engined [Formula Nippon](/wiki/Formula_Nippon "Formula Nippon") teams and also [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") [Super GT](/wiki/Super_GT "Super GT") teams in GT500 and GT300 cars. In 2009, Valencia CF became an official partner of former 250cc team Stop And Go Racing Team and in 2014 of [Aspar Team](/wiki/Aspar_Team "Aspar Team") in [MotoGP](/wiki/MotoGP "MotoGP"), [Moto2](/wiki/Moto2 "Moto2") and [Moto3](/wiki/Moto3 "Moto3") classes, respectively.

## Esports involvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Valencia_CF&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Esports involvement")]

In June 2016, Valencia opened an [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") division with presences in *[Hearthstone](/wiki/Hearthstone "Hearthstone")*, *[Rocket League](/wiki/Rocket_League "Rocket League")* and *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")* – in the last case, they joined [Beşiktaş](/wiki/Be%C5%9Fikta%C5%9F_J.K. "Beşiktaş J.K."), [Santos](/wiki/Santos_FC "Santos FC"), [Schalke](/wiki/FC_Schalke_04 "FC Schalke 04") and [PSG](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_eSports "Paris Saint-Germain eSports") in acquiring *League* teams. They announced their *League* roster on 13 July, composed mostly of Spanish players, including some with [European League of Legends Championship Series](/wiki/European_League_of_Legends_Championship_Series "European League of Legends Championship Series") (EU LCS) experience.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

In November 2020, Valencia CF eSports launched a team on *[Arena of Valor](/wiki/Arena_of_Valor "Arena of Valor")* in Thailand*.* The team consist of six Thai players, competing in the RoV Pro League competitions. They joined the local club [Buriram United FC](/wiki/Buriram_United_F.C. "Buriram United F.C."), and after that, French club [Paris Saint-Germain FC](/wiki/PSG_Esports "PSG Esports") in acquiring AoV teams.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
